section.title: The Resurrection
section.sectionRationale: At dawn on the first day of the week the women come with spices and find the stone rolled away and the tomb empty. Two men in radiant apparel ask why they seek the living among the dead and declare that He has risen, reminding them of Jesus' own words in Galilee. The women remember, and run to tell the Eleven, who dismiss it as nonsense, though Peter runs to the tomb and goes away wondering. The resurrection dawns quietly, on the word of angels and the memory of Jesus' promise.
section.thesis: Jesus is risen exactly as He said, so the empty tomb is not a riddle but the fulfillment of His own word, calling His people to stop seeking the living among the dead and to remember and believe what He foretold.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the women at the empty tomb and the angels' announcement; and the report to the Eleven with Peter running to see.

units.label: The empty tomb and the angels' word
units.note: why do you look for the living among the dead

verses.text: On the first day of the week, very early in the morning, the women came to the tomb, bringing the spices they had prepared.
They found the stone rolled away from the tomb,
but when they entered, they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus.
While they were puzzling over this, suddenly two men in radiant apparel stood beside them.
As the women bowed their faces to the ground in terror, the two men asked them, “Why do you look for the {{zao|living}} among the dead?
He is not here; He has {{egeiro|risen}}! Remember how He told you while He was still in Galilee:
‘The Son of Man {{dei|must}} be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and on the third day rise again.’”
And when they returned from the tomb, they reported all these things to the Eleven and to all the others.
It was Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the other women with them who told this to the apostles.
But their words seemed like nonsense to them, and they did not believe the women.
Peter, however, got up and ran to the tomb. And after bending down and seeing only the linen cloths, he went away, wondering to himself what had happened.

zao.gloss: To live, to be alive. The angels' question, why do you look for the living among the dead, announces that Jesus belongs no longer among the dead but among the living. He is the Living One, death behind Him, and the tomb is empty because life has conquered.

egeiro.gloss: To raise up, to rouse from sleep or death. He has been raised, the angels declare, the passive pointing to the Father who raised His Son. The resurrection is God's mighty act of vindication, reversing the verdict of the cross and inaugurating the new creation.

dei.gloss: It is necessary, it must be. The Son of Man must be delivered, crucified, and rise, a divine necessity fixed in God's plan and foretold in Scripture. The cross and resurrection were no accident but the appointed way of salvation, exactly as Jesus had said.

mimnesko.gloss: To remember, to call to mind. The angels do not offer new information but recall Jesus' own words, and the women remember. Faith in the resurrection is grounded in remembering what Jesus foretold; His word interprets the empty tomb.

basic.context: Very early on Sunday, the women who had watched Jesus die come to the tomb with spices to finish His burial. They find the stone rolled away and the body gone. As they stand bewildered, two men in dazzling clothes appear and ask why they are seeking the living among the dead. He is not here, they say, He has risen, and they remind the women of what Jesus taught in Galilee: that the Son of Man must be crucified and on the third day rise. The women remember His words and hurry back to tell the Eleven and the others. But the report sounds like nonsense to the apostles, and they do not believe. Only Peter runs to the tomb, sees the empty linen cloths, and goes away wondering what has happened.
basic.hermeneutics: Luke grounds the resurrection not in speculation but in the word of Jesus recalled by the angels. The empty tomb is interpreted by His prior teaching: this had to happen, and He said it would. The disbelief of the apostles underlines that faith comes by remembering and trusting His word, not by natural expectation.
basic.originalLanguages: The angels ask why they seek the zao, the living, among the dead, for Jesus has egeiro, been raised. This happened because the Son of Man dei, must, suffer and rise, a divine necessity. And faith begins as the women mimnesko, remember, His words.
basic.typology: The third-day rising fulfills the pattern of Jonah and the promise of Scripture. The first day of the week becomes the day of new creation, the dawn of God's new world in the risen Christ. The women as first witnesses fulfill the reversal by which God chooses the lowly to proclaim His greatest deed.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Living One who has conquered death, raised by the Father exactly as He foretold. He is the Son of Man whose suffering and rising were a divine necessity written in Scripture, and the risen Lord whose own word is the key that unlocks the meaning of the empty tomb.
basic.god: God is the one who raised Jesus, vindicating His Son and reversing the cross. He keeps His word to the letter, so that what Jesus promised has come to pass. He chooses faithful women as the first heralds of the resurrection, honoring the lowly and the overlooked.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 15 lists the resurrection appearances and calls it of first importance. Luke 9 records Jesus foretelling His death and rising. Psalm 16 promises that God will not let His Holy One see decay. Acts 2 preaches that God raised Jesus, freeing Him from the pangs of death.
basic.oneStory: The tomb is empty because the crucified King is alive, raised as He said. The story that ran through the cross now breaks into dawn, and the first word of the new creation is an invitation to remember Jesus' promise and believe, to stop seeking the living among the dead.

context.literary: Luke's resurrection account opens the final chapter, which moves from the empty tomb to Emmaus to the appearance to the Eleven and the ascension. The stress throughout is on the fulfillment of Scripture and Jesus' own words. The women's faithful witness and the apostles' initial unbelief set up the pattern of the whole chapter: eyes and minds must be opened to believe.
context.historical: Women came to complete burial rites with spices after the Sabbath rest. In that culture their testimony carried little legal weight, which makes their role as first witnesses a mark of the account's authenticity. The rolled stone, the empty tomb, and the folded linen cloths are concrete details. Peter's run to the tomb reflects the disciples' confusion and dawning hope.
context.canonical: The resurrection is the foundation of the apostolic gospel, preached in Acts and expounded in the epistles. The third-day rising according to the Scriptures ties the event to the whole Old Testament pattern of death and vindication. The first day of the week becomes the Lord's Day, the church's weekly celebration of the new creation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To testify that Jesus truly rose from the dead on the third day as He had foretold, to ground resurrection faith in remembering His word and the witness of Scripture, and to show that even His closest followers came to believe not by expectation but by evidence and recollection.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account describes the historical event of the resurrection. Its abiding summons is prescriptive in effect: do not seek the living among the dead, remember and believe the word of Jesus, and receive the risen Lord as the apostles finally did.

debates.question: Why does Luke highlight the women as the first witnesses?

views.label: A mark of historical authenticity and divine reversal
views.case: In that culture a woman's testimony was not highly valued, so no one inventing the story would choose women as the first witnesses; their prominence points to the truthfulness of the account. It also fits Luke's theme that God exalts the lowly, entrusting the greatest news to the overlooked.

views.label: The reward of faithful presence
views.case: The women had followed Jesus, watched the crucifixion, and marked the tomb, and now they are first to hear He is risen. Their faithful love through the darkness is honored with the first announcement of the dawn, a pattern of grace to the devoted.

debates.bearing: Either way the women's witness underlines both the reliability of the resurrection and God's way of honoring the humble and faithful with the good news of the risen Christ.

debates.question: Why did the apostles not believe the women's report at first?

views.label: Resurrection was not their expectation despite Jesus' words
views.case: Though Jesus had foretold His rising, the disciples did not grasp it, so the news struck them as nonsense. Their unbelief shows that faith in the resurrection did not arise from wishful thinking or expectation but had to be created by evidence and by remembering His word.

views.label: A sober honesty that strengthens the testimony
views.case: Luke's frank record of the apostles' doubt, and Peter's puzzled run to the tomb, would not be invented by those wishing to prove a triumph. The reluctance to believe adds credibility, showing that the witnesses were not gullible but had to be convinced.

debates.bearing: Both readings show that resurrection faith rests on the word and the evidence, not on prior expectation, and that even honest doubt gave way before the risen Lord.

study.originalLanguages: The angels' question turns everything: why do you look for the zao, the Living One, among the dead? Jesus has egeiro, been raised, the passive form pointing to the Father who raised Him, God's mighty act of vindication that reverses the verdict of the cross. And this was no accident, for the Son of Man dei, it was necessary, must suffer and be crucified and rise on the third day, a divine necessity fixed in God's saving plan and written in Scripture. The turning point for the women is not new information but memory: they mimnesko, they remembered, His words, and so the empty tomb is read rightly only in the light of what Jesus had said. Faith in the resurrection is faith in the word of the risen Lord.

typology.type: Jonah three days in the fish and delivered
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man rising on the third day as He foretold, Luke 24:6-7.

typology.type: The first day of creation, light breaking the darkness
typology.fulfillment: The first day of the week, the dawn of new creation in the risen Christ, Luke 24:1.

typology.type: God's choice of the lowly to bear His word
typology.fulfillment: Faithful women made the first witnesses of the resurrection, Luke 24:9-10.

study.christ: Jesus is the Living One, no longer to be sought among the dead, for He has burst the tomb. He is the Son of Man whose suffering and rising were a divine necessity, foretold by Him in Galilee and written in the Scriptures, so that His resurrection is the keeping of His own sure word. Raised by the Father, He is vindicated as the crucified King whose death was not defeat but the appointed path to glory. And He is the risen Lord whose word, recalled by the angels and remembered by the women, is the very light in which the empty tomb makes sense. To believe He is risen is to trust the One who said He would be.
study.god: The God of the resurrection is the Father who raised Jesus from the dead, reversing the cross and declaring His Son vindicated and victorious. He is the God who keeps His word to the letter, so that what Jesus foretold in Galilee comes to pass at the tomb, and the necessity He fixed in His plan is fulfilled. He is the God who honors the lowly, entrusting the first proclamation of the greatest news in history to faithful women whose testimony the world would discount. And He is the God who patiently draws even doubting disciples toward faith, meeting their unbelief with evidence and with the memory of His Son's promise.

crossReferences.note: Christ died, was buried, and was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, the apostolic summary of the gospel.
Jesus foretold that the Son of Man must suffer, be killed, and on the third day be raised, the word the angels recall.
You will not abandon My soul to Sheol or let Your Holy One see decay, the promise fulfilled in the resurrection.
God raised Him up, freeing Him from the agony of death, the apostolic proclamation of the empty tomb.

study.oneStory: The empty tomb at dawn is the turning point of the whole story: the crucified King is alive, raised by the Father exactly as He said. What ran through betrayal, cross, and grave now breaks into the light of the first day, and the first word of the new creation is a summons to remember Jesus' promise and believe. The women run with the news, the apostles struggle toward faith, and the risen Lord Himself will soon meet them to open their eyes and their minds.

quiz.q: What do the women find when they come to the tomb?

options.text: The stone rolled away and the body of the Lord Jesus gone.
options.note: Luke 24:2-3 records the rolled stone and the missing body.

options.text: That the tomb had been disturbed overnight.
options.note: True in a sense, but it misses the specific finding of the empty tomb and absent body.

options.text: The body of Jesus still wrapped inside.
options.note: The body was gone; only the linen cloths remained, as Peter later saw.

options.text: Soldiers still guarding a sealed tomb.
options.note: They found the stone rolled away and the tomb open and empty.

quiz.q: What do the two men in radiant apparel announce?

options.text: He is not here; He has risen, just as He foretold in Galilee.
options.note: Luke 24:6-7 proclaims the resurrection and recalls Jesus' own prediction.

options.text: That Jesus is alive and no longer in the tomb.
options.note: True, but it omits the crucial link to Jesus' own foretelling of His rising.

options.text: That the body had been taken elsewhere.
options.note: The opposite of the angels' message; He has risen, not been relocated.

options.text: That the women should keep the matter secret.
options.note: The women went and reported it; there was no command to stay silent.

quiz.q: Why do the angels tell the women to remember Jesus' words?

options.text: Because the resurrection fulfills exactly what Jesus foretold, so His word interprets the empty tomb.
options.note: Luke 24:6-8 grounds resurrection faith in remembering Jesus' prediction.

options.text: So the women would recall that Jesus had spoken of rising.
options.note: True, but it omits that His word is the key that makes sense of the empty tomb.

options.text: To test whether the women had paid attention.
options.note: The point is not a memory test but that His word explains the event.

options.text: Because the angels had no news of their own to give.
options.note: They proclaim He has risen; recalling His word confirms and interprets it.

quiz.q: How do the apostles first respond to the women's report?

options.text: They dismiss it as nonsense and do not believe, though Peter runs to see the tomb.
options.note: Luke 24:11-12 records the disbelief and Peter's wondering run to the tomb.

options.text: They do not believe the women's account.
options.note: True, but it omits Peter's response of running to the tomb to check.

options.text: They immediately rejoice and believe.
options.note: The opposite; the news seemed like nonsense to them at first.

options.text: They rebuke the women for spreading rumors.
options.note: They disbelieve, but there is no rebuke; Peter goes to investigate.

quiz.q: How does Luke show the resurrection was no accident but God's plan?

options.text: The angels say the Son of Man must be crucified and rise on the third day, a divine necessity Jesus had foretold.
options.note: Luke 24:7 frames the events as a necessity fixed in God's plan and Jesus' word.

options.text: The events happened just as Jesus had predicted.
options.note: True, but it omits the language of necessity, that it must happen.

options.text: The empty tomb surprised even the angels.
options.note: The angels proclaim it confidently as the fulfillment of God's plan.

options.text: The resurrection took God's plan in an unexpected direction.
options.note: It was the appointed path, foretold and necessary, not a detour.

questions: The angels asked, why do you look for the living among the dead? Where are you still seeking life in things that cannot give it, when the risen Christ is alive?
Faith dawned as the women remembered Jesus' words. Which of Christ's promises have you forgotten that would change everything if you truly remembered them?
The resurrection was a divine necessity, foretold in Scripture. How does it steady your faith to know the cross and empty tomb were God's plan all along, not a rescue improvised at the last moment?
The apostles first heard the good news as nonsense. Are there truths about the risen Christ you have half dismissed rather than run, like Peter, to see for yourself?
God entrusted the first news of the resurrection to faithful women the world would discount. Where might God be calling you, ordinary and overlooked, to carry the news that Jesus is alive?

translationNotes.note: 'On the first day of the week' translates a Greek phrase literally reading 'on the first of the Sabbaths', marking the day after the Sabbath. This first day becomes the Lord's Day, the weekly celebration of the resurrection.
'He has risen' renders a passive verb that points to God the Father as the one who raised Jesus. The resurrection is God's act of vindicating His Son, a truth the apostles preach throughout Acts.
The word rendered 'must' expresses divine necessity. The suffering and rising of the Son of Man were fixed in God's saving plan and foretold in Scripture and by Jesus Himself, not a tragic accident.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 24:1-12. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
